bibliographic.citation: Joseph Hall, 'Short Pieces from MS. Cotton Galba E. IX,' Englische Studien 21 (1895), 201-209.
bibliographic.title: Short Pieces from MS. Cotton Galba E. IX

entities.description: Hall's edited selection of three numbered units from Cotton Galba E IX, containing four intellectually distinct Middle English poems. The edition prints manuscript folio and column references, italic expansions and supplied readings, glosses, emendations, and collations against Ritson and Wright for Sir Peny.
entities.label: Joseph Hall, Short Pieces from MS. Cotton Galba E. IX (1895)

rights.basis: Published in 1895; U-M also labels the exact digital item No Copyright - United States.
rights.component: 1895 article text and editorial matter

access.accessMethod: Register for a Reader Pass and request the item through the British Library's online collection item request form; all manuscripts are consulted in London.
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff. 3-114
notes: The current record says a letter of introduction is required.

access.accessMethod: No complete public digital object or digitised-content link was exposed in the current exact item record; use Imaging Services for a mediated whole-object reproduction request.
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff. 3-114

access.accessMethod: Submit the British Library online digitisation order form using the shelfmark. For a complete physical-source surrogate, ask whether f. 2 is currently part of the same bound object, and request f. 2 plus ff. 3-114 when physically applicable, front and back boards or covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every manuscript leaf recto and verso, edges when offered, and any inserted, detached, or separately housed matter the repository can reproduce. Request full color from the original where permitted. State separately that the work occupies ff. 48vb-49rb and 50vb-51vb.
access.contact: permissions@bl.uk for copying or reproduction permission; licensing@bl.uk for publication questions
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff. 3-114
access.format: Repository-supplied digital photographs or available surrogate scans
notes: British Library guidance says original manuscripts are photographed rather than scanned and asks requesters to specify recto, verso, and color requirements.
A request for only 'the work' would omit most of the manuscript and would not satisfy complete physical-source coverage.
Large, fragile, or difficult material may require individual discussion and pricing.

rights.basis: The current Imaging Services terms say supplied material may carry intellectual-property rights and prohibit copying or reproduction without written permission.
rights.component: Images supplied through British Library Imaging Services
notes: Ordering a reference facsimile does not itself grant permission to publish it in the Git repository or in commercial print-on-demand products.
Ask the British Library for written terms covering the intended repository and publication uses before redistribution.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.value: First quarter of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A parchment composite codex of three originally independent manuscripts associated at an early stage. Hall's discontiguous selection lies near the end of Part I: the transitoriness poem at ff. 48vb-49rb, Sir Peny at ff. 50vb-51rb, and the two Passion poems at f. 51va-b; the Prophecies of Merlin intervenes at ff. 49rb-50vb.

holding.shelfmark: Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff 3-114

identifiers.value: Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff 3-114

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff. 3-114

physicalDescription.extent: Main cataloged item ff. 3-114; a separately cataloged Psalter leaf is f. 2
physicalDescription.format: Composite codex in three early-associated parts: ff. 3-51, 52-75, and 76-114
notes: Part I contains the four selected poems amid other Middle English works; the selection is not a continuous manuscript anthology.
The Database of Middle English Romance records Northern origin for the witness. This is witness-production evidence, not a common composition place for the poems.
The British Library record requires a letter of introduction for physical consultation.

rights.basis: The anonymous texts are attested in manuscripts made no later than the early fifteenth century.
rights.component: Underlying medieval poem texts
notes: This does not determine rights in modern editions, encodings, catalog text, photographs, or repository-supplied images.

rights.basis: The current Imaging Services terms say supplied material may carry intellectual-property rights and prohibit copying or reproduction without written permission.
rights.component: Images supplied through British Library Imaging Services
notes: Ordering a reference facsimile does not itself grant permission to publish it in the Git repository or in commercial print-on-demand products.
Ask the British Library for written terms covering the intended repository and publication uses before redistribution.

bibliographic.citation: Joseph Ritson, Pieces of Ancient Popular Poetry from Authentic Manuscripts and Old Printed Copies, 2nd ed. (London: William Pickering, 1833), 103-108.
bibliographic.title: Pieces of Ancient Popular Poetry from Authentic Manuscripts and Old Printed Copies

entities.description: The first known printing of the Cotton Sir Peny text. The prefatory note says Ritson had intended it for the collection; the posthumous second edition prints it from Cotton Galba E IX, then numbered fol. 47b, and notes a differing 45-line fragment in Caius MS 174. At line 13 it supplies 'ofte' from the Caius witness.
entities.label: Sir Peny in Ritson's Pieces of Ancient Popular Poetry, second edition (1833)

rights.basis: Published in 1833.
rights.component: 1833 printed edition text

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, ed., The Latin Poems Commonly Attributed to Walter Mapes (London: Camden Society, 1841), 359-361.
bibliographic.publisher: J. B. Nichols and Son for the Camden Society
bibliographic.title: The Latin Poems Commonly Attributed to Walter Mapes

entities.description: Thomas Wright's appendix printing of the Cotton text, headed 'English, of the Fourteenth Century.' Wright identifies Cotton Galba E IX, then numbered fol. 47v, mentions the differing Caius copy, and states that the Cotton ballad had already appeared in Ritson's second edition.
entities.label: Sir Penny in Wright's Latin Poems Commonly Attributed to Walter Mapes (1841)

rights.basis: Published in 1841.
rights.component: 1841 printed edition text

access.accessMethod: Ask the College Library for a complete color surrogate of the whole surviving MS 174/95, including binding, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and every recto and verso; identify Sir Peny at manuscript pp. 484-486 separately without limiting the whole-object request.
access.entityLabel: Cambridge, Gonville and Caius College, MS 174/95
access.format: Repository-supplied images or onsite consultation; exact service and price require enquiry
access.provider: Gonville and Caius College Library, University of Cambridge

rights.basis: No item-specific image delivery or reuse terms have been obtained. The College copyright policy says supplied material is normally for private research and requires written application and license terms for publication.
rights.component: Any Gonville and Caius College supplied manuscript photographs
rights.jurisdiction: College terms and applicable copyright law

entities.description: A parallel, substantially differing 93-line witness of Sir Peny at pp. 484-486. It is not Hall's base text, but Ritson's edition uses its reading 'ofte' at Cotton line 13 and Hall reports that indirect supply.
entities.label: Cambridge, Gonville and Caius College, MS 174/95

notes: DIMEV records 93 lines and the manuscript title 'Explicit de dynario Johanne magistro.'

rights.basis: No item-specific image delivery or reuse terms have been obtained. The College copyright policy says supplied material is normally for private research and requires written application and license terms for publication.
rights.component: Any Gonville and Caius College supplied manuscript photographs
rights.jurisdiction: College terms and applicable copyright law

entities.description: A fourteenth-century parchment codex of sermons and religious material. DIMEV indexes parallel versions of both Passion poems together at ff. 165vb-166ra: the appeal begins at 165vb and is followed by a five-stanza version of the second poem.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Don. c. 13

physicalDescription.extent: At least 168 leaves; relevant additions at ff. 165vb-166ra
notes: The Digital Bodleian object publishes only fol. 162v, so it does not show either related poem.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Second half of the fourteenth century

entities.description: A parchment codex of the second half of the fourteenth century containing the Prick of Conscience and religious and moral poems. DIMEV indexes the first Passion poem at f. 80rb and the second at f. 93va, separated within the manuscript.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. poet. 175

notes: The repository catalog groups the relevant material under 'Religious and moral poems.'

next_steps: Submit the whole-object Imaging Services request scope recorded in access:bl-cotton-reproduction.

Ask the repository to clarify whether separately cataloged f. 2 is part of the same present physical object and whether covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and detached matter can be photographed.
Record the quote, delivered coverage, resolution, color, terms, and exact work folio-to-image mapping when the repository responds.
openQuestions.question: Can the British Library supply a complete color surrogate of the entire current Cotton MS Galba E IX physical object, including f. 2 when physically bound with ff. 3-114 and all binding and endmatter surfaces?

next_steps: Ask permissions@bl.uk and licensing@bl.uk for terms covering public Git redistribution, scholarly quotation, and commercial or noncommercial publication.
Keep supplied images in the ignored research cache unless written permission authorizes redistribution.
Separate permission to study the images from permission to publish or redistribute them.
openQuestions.question: What written permission and fees would apply to sharing any British Library-supplied Cotton facsimile in the Git repository or using its images in commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand publications?

next_steps: Consult Hall's papers, correspondence, and fuller editorial statements if provenance at that granularity becomes necessary.
Retain the direct-transcription relation as strongly supported rather than documentarily proven.
openQuestions.question: Did Hall work directly from Cotton Galba E IX throughout, or from an unrecorded transcript or photographs for any portion?

next_steps: Ask DIMEV to clarify the record note.
Preserve the separate IMEV 110 and 2080 boundaries and Hall's combined print structure in parallel.
openQuestions.question: Does DIMEV record 201's '54 lines' note intentionally describe Hall's combined third unit rather than the 18-line poem delimited by that record's incipit and explicit?

next_steps: Request complete-codex imaging quotes from the holding libraries, naming the target pages or folios separately.
Do not treat Digital Bodleian's single Don. c. 13 image at fol. 162v as target-text or complete-codex coverage.
Cache any authorized public complete objects and record exact target canvas ranges.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public or requestable color facsimiles be obtained for Gonville and Caius MS 174/95, Bodleian MS. Don. c. 13, and Bodleian MS. Rawl. poet. 175?

reviewNotes: The immediate source relationship was checked against the complete BSB journal scan rather than inferred from U-M metadata alone.
Hall's three numbered units and the four-work DIMEV boundary are preserved simultaneously instead of forcing one structure to erase the other.
Current Cotton foliation is used for the witness; Ritson's and Wright's old fol. 47b/v references are retained only as historical locators.

Complete-source caching is object-relative: the Hall cache contains all 492 journal-volume canvases, while the Don. c. 13 cache contains all one canvas of a provider-selected object but remains partial coverage of the manuscript.

lineage.summary: CME00081 is the repository copy of the University of Michigan encoding of Joseph Hall's 'Short Pieces from MS. Cotton Galba E. IX,' printed in Englische Studien 21 (1895), pp. 201-209. Hall directly edited selected passages from British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff. 48vb-49rb and 50vb-51vb, and collated Sir Peny against Joseph Ritson's 1833 and Thomas Wright's 1841 printings. Hall numbered three pieces, but DIMEV 1.0 separates his final unit into two poems; the selection therefore contains four medieval works. A complete 492-canvas scan of the entire 1895 journal volume, complete scans of the Ritson and Wright volumes, and the complete DIMEV 1.0 data release are cached locally. The Cotton manuscript has no exposed public digital object in its current British Library record, so the manifest records a whole-object Imaging Services order scope and maps the two discontiguous work ranges separately. One parallel Bodleian witness has a one-image Digital Bodleian object at fol. 162v; it is cached as a complete capture of that published selection but is partial manuscript coverage and does not overlap the relevant texts at ff. 165vb-166ra.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous authors (four poems; common authorship is not asserted)
catalogSummary.date: Constituent composition dates are mostly unresolved; All Is But a Phantom is estimated c. 1350, the source witness dates 1400-1425, and the exact selection was first published in 1895
catalogSummary.region: Places of composition unresolved; the source witness is associated with Northern England, which is not transferred to the poems' composition
catalogSummary.title: Short Pieces from MS. Cotton Galba E. IX

catalogingSubject.label: Short Pieces from MS. Cotton Galba E. IX
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Joseph Hall's exact 1895 edited selection from British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff. 3-114, not the complete manuscript and not one medieval authorial work. Hall numbered three units, but modern DIMEV records show that his third unit joins two distinct Middle English poems under separate Latin verse frames. The selection therefore contains four anonymous medieval poems in three Hall-numbered divisions.

contentParts.label: I. In imagine pertransit homo / All Is But a Phantom
notes: DIMEV record 339; IMEV/NIMEV 189.
contentParts.summary: A moral and religious poem on transience, hoarded wealth, charity to the poor, death, judgment, and salvation.

contentParts.label: II. Sir Peny
notes: DIMEV record 2489; IMEV/NIMEV 1480. The 93-line Caius copy is a substantially different parallel version, not Hall's base text.
contentParts.summary: A personification satire on money's power over rulers, clergy, courts, social rank, friendship, life, and salvation, ending with counsel to spend wealth charitably.

contentParts.label: III. Popule meus, quid feci tibi?
notes: The four Latin L elements plus 54 Middle English L elements total 58.
contentParts.summary: Hall's third numbered unit, containing two distinct Middle English Passion poems, each preceded by a Latin couplet.

contentParts.label: Appeal of Christ to Man (Bides a while and haldes youre pais)

locators.value: DIV2 3, first Latin couplet and Middle English lines 1-18

notes: DIMEV record 201; IMEV/NIMEV 110. Metric includes the two preceding Latin verse lines and 18 Middle English lines.
contentParts.summary: Christ asks passers-by to pause, behold his wounded body on the Cross, cease sin, and trust in his mercy.

contentParts.label: How Christ Speaks to Sinful Man of His Great Mercy

locators.value: DIV2 3, second Latin couplet and Middle English lines 19-54

notes: DIMEV record 3387; IMEV/NIMEV 2080. Metric includes the two preceding Latin verse lines and 36 Middle English lines.
contentParts.summary: A six-stanza appeal from Christ on the Cross promising mercy and salvation to the penitent sinner, with Mary Magdalene and Peter as examples.

assertion.notes: PCMEP supplies 1350 as an approximate composition date; it is not converted into an invented exact normalized interval.
This dates the surviving Cotton copies, not the poems' composition and not Hall's artificial selection as a medieval unit.

dateStatements.display: 1400-1425 in Cotton MS Galba E IX

assertion.notes: The 1833 second edition says Ritson had intended the poem for the collection and that it is 'now given'; this supports 1833 rather than the collection's 1791 first edition as this poem's first publication.
Hall states that his first and third numbered pieces were printed there for the first time; his exact three-unit selection first appears in this article.

extent.description: Three Hall-numbered DIV2 units, but four medieval poem identities. The XML has 275 L elements in 48 LG or Q groups; the final DIV2 has four Latin and 54 Middle English lines.

formStatements.description: All four constituent Middle English works and the four embedded Latin frame lines are verse; the XML contains 275 L elements in 48 LG or Q groups.

assertion.notes: Two Latin verse lines precede each of the two Middle English poems nested in Hall's third division.

notes: The preferred title is Hall's established edition title, while the unit type and scope note prevent it from being mistaken for a medieval collection title.
The XML's TEXT LANG value includes English because Hall's editorial headnote and notes are in modern English; modern editorial prose is not cataloged as a language of the medieval selected works.
The source witness's Northern origin is recorded in lineage but not asserted as the poems' common place of composition.
Any manuscript surrogate should capture the whole physical Cotton MS Galba E IX object first and then map the discontiguous target portions at ff. 48vb-49rb and 50vb-51vb.

next_steps: Consult current poem-specific linguistic and textual studies for IMEV 189, 1480, 110, and 2080.
Keep composition separate from the 1400-1425 date and Northern origin of the Cotton witness.
Do not assign a single composition region to Hall's artificial selection without part-level evidence.
openQuestions.question: When and where were Sir Peny and the two Passion poems composed, and does current scholarship refine PCMEP's approximate 1350 date for All Is But a Phantom?

next_steps: Treat the 54-line source note as likely coverage of Hall's combined third unit until DIMEV clarifies it.
Retain the two-work boundary established by separate DIMEV records, incipits, Latin frames, and parallel witnesses.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV record 201 describe the Cotton witness as 54 lines when its incipit, explicit, and Hall's structure identify the first 18 Middle English lines separately from DIMEV 3387's following 36 lines?

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous poets of the four selected poems

scope.description: Anonymous authorship of the four medieval poems, without a claim of shared authorship

assertion.notes: Hall prints manuscript folio and column milestones and repeatedly distinguishes manuscript readings, omissions, additions, and corrections.

scope.description: Transcription of the selected Cotton manuscript passages
Collation against Ritson's 1833 and Wright's 1841 printings, including a reading indirectly supplied from Gonville and Caius MS 174

subjects.label: Transience of the world and worldly vanity
Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment

summaries.value: Joseph Hall's 1895 selection of anonymous Middle English verse from Cotton MS Galba E IX juxtaposes a moral poem on worldly transience, the personified-money satire Sir Peny, and two Passion lyrics in which Christ appeals from the Cross to the sinner. Hall numbered the final two lyrics as one third piece beneath the heading 'Popule meus, quid feci tibi?' and linked them with two separate Latin couplets; modern DIMEV records establish the four-work boundary retained here.

Three Hall-numbered divisions on printed pp. 201-209 contain 275 encoded verse lines: 94 Middle English lines in item I; 123 Middle English lines in item II; and 58 lines in item III, comprising four Latin framing lines and 54 Middle English lines. Intellectually, item III contains an 18-line appeal of Christ and a 36-line poem on Christ's mercy.

titles.value: Short Pieces from MS. Cotton Galba E. IX

assertion.notes: Hall heads the poem with the Latin phrase 'In imagine pertransit homo'; the English discovery title follows modern scholarly usage.

titles.value: Bides a while and haldes youre pais
Man, thus on rode I hing for thee

work.summary: Joseph Hall's 1895 selection of anonymous Middle English verse from Cotton MS Galba E IX juxtaposes a moral poem on worldly transience, the personified-money satire Sir Peny, and two Passion lyrics in which Christ appeals from the Cross to the sinner. Hall numbered the final two lyrics as one third piece beneath the heading 'Popule meus, quid feci tibi?' and linked them with two separate Latin couplets; modern DIMEV records establish the four-work boundary retained here.
